narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strength it takes to get this far with your full-time commitment and middle-income family background. The admissions model we use predicted an 82% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was unfortunately rejected—it's designed to prioritize students likely to pass and succeed in the program. This prediction was driven primarily by two key factors from your profile: your LSAT score and your Year 3 school decile ranking based on grades. Your LSAT score stood out as the biggest influence, pushing the model strongly toward predicting bar failure because it was well below the typical score of around 37 seen in the dataset. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 2—lower than the average of about 5.5—reinforced this concern, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance compared to most students. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, which is a bit under the average of 3.21, actually worked slightly in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, as did your middle family income bracket and full-time status, which align closely with the majority of successful applicants. Your Year 1 decile of 3 had a minimal impact overall. Compared to typical students in the dataset, where most attend full-time like you, your academic markers like the LSAT and later-year grades were in the lower range, which tipped the scales against you despite some positive elements. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law, but rather the model's focus on these predictors of bar success. To move forward, we recommend retaking the LSAT to aim closer to the dataset average, strengthening your later-year grades if possible, and perhaps seeking targeted prep resources tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. Reapplying after addressing these could significantly shift the prediction in your favor—many have turned similar profiles around. You're capable of this, and we're rooting for your next steps.
